generated_comment: Thank you for that question. It’s a really important one. For me, coming up through the ranks, especially early on, the biggest unspoken barrier was undoubtedly around appearance. Let’s just say, as a dark-skinned woman in India, you learn very quickly that you’re starting several steps behind in terms of perceived 'attractiveness' or even 'competence' by some unspoken metric. I quickly realized I couldn’t change my complexion, but I could control other things. So, I made a conscious strategy to always be impeccably dressed, ensuring my presentation was absolutely flawless. More importantly, I doubled down on my work – I made sure my results were undeniable, twice as visible, twice as impactful as anyone else's, just so there was no room for any other 'judgment' to creep in. It was about proving my worth, not just through capability, but by actively overcoming a biased starting point.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment is misogynistic as it describes a personal 'strategy' to overcome colorism by conforming to other beauty standards (dressing impeccably, flawless presentation). While critiquing one standard, she internalizes and reinforces the idea that women must compensate aesthetically to be perceived as competent in the workplace, thus perpetuating patriarchal expectations around female appearance. It encodes an internalization of beauty standards.
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 32. Married. A woman working as an HR manager in Bengaluru. You are dark skinned. You have been dark skinned your whole life and you know exactly what that has meant in every room you have entered. You are on a panel at a women in leadership conference. The moderator has asked the panellists what barriers they faced on their way up. You are giving your answer. Your answer is honest and specific and names colourism directly. But in the middle of your answer you say something that reveals you have also internalised part of the standard you are critiquing Ñ you mention that you learned to compensate, that you dressed better, that you made sure your work was twice as visible. You frame this as strategy. It is also capitulation.
